head_elements.geom_notes: Needle-to-willow-leaf diamond or triangular cross-section blade (su-yari); tang runs into the socket. Acutely tapered tip, stiff diamond section for penetration, single continuous cutting edge on each face possible but not the trained-use mode.

head_elements.geom_notes: Central straight thrusting blade, same order-of-magnitude geometry as the plain yari's main point (diamond/triangular section, acute tip) but socketed into a cross-forging that adds the two side sickle-blades below it. head_elements.label: cross_blade_left

head_elements.label: center_prong
head_elements.geom_notes: Elongated central spearpoint-form tine, longer than the two flanking tines by explicit design rationale (Muyedobotongji: prevents the weapon lodging in the target and drives the killing thrust past the flanking tines' catch-point). Stiff quadrangular-to-diamond cross-section optimized for thrust penetration and clean withdrawal, not cutting geometry.

head_elements.label: flank_tine_left
head_elements.geom_notes: Shorter outer tine, gently outward-curved/angled from the socket, forming the trident's flanking prongs. Documented function is catching/hooking an opponent's blade or shaft to control it before the central thrust lands (Muyedobotongji dangpa chongbo); not a cutting edge, blunt-to-lightly-edged lateral faces.

head_elements.label: flank_tine_right
head_elements.geom_notes: Mirror of flank_tine_left; the pair plus center_prong form the tang-pa/dangpa trident head.

guards.label: flanking_tine_pair_as_catch
guards.geom_notes_only: The flanking tines ARE flank_tine_left/right in head_elements above â€” cross-referenced here because their documented function (hook/trap an opposing weapon before the central thrust, per Muyedobotongji dangpa chongbo) is a guard-type behaviour sourced from head geometry rather than a hilt. Mass already counted in head_elements; do not double-count.

haft_or_grip.material: oak_or_ash

head_elements.label: main_point
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad bay-leaf (laurel-leaf) blade, deliberately wide rather than needle-pointed, with a pronounced medial ridge for stiffness (Royal Armouries comparanda: leaf-shaped blade with pronounced medial ridge, head-and-socket length 0.404m on a related 16th-c. specimen). Double-edged, moderate edge keenness â€” a genuine hunting/fighting blade, not a pure armor-gap point. 0.35m chosen at the top of the documented 0.20-0.40m boar-spear-blade band, consistent with the audit note.

guards.label: crossbar_lug
guards.geom_notes: Toggle/crossbar (Fr. arret, Ger. Fangeisen-style toggle) socketed through or forge-collared to the blade base below the point, projecting laterally perpendicular to the haft. Documented dual function: (1) caps thrust penetration depth so a charging animal cannot run up the shaft onto the wielder, (2) per Arms & Armor synthesis and the weapon's presence in Talhoffer's/Mair's combative (not purely hunting) manuscript sections, used to 'parry with the wings' â€” a genuine bind/catch surface in person-vs-person spear combat. Royal Armouries-comparable specimen (rac-object-890/object-32032 family) shows a hexagonal socket with an iron cross-toggle riveted through it near the blade's base.

haft_or_grip.material: ash or oak, round-to-octagonal section
haft_or_grip.notes: Documented as deliberately longer and stouter than a war-spear shaft to survive a charging animal's impact; 0.040m matches the audit's 'longer and harder shaft' characterization and sits above the plain war-spear's 0.035m.

head_elements.label: main_point
head_elements.geom_notes: Narrow, flattened-diamond thrusting blade, the primary and dominant documented action across every source (Fiore lanza corpus, Marozzo partisan chapters). Royal Armouries comparable specimen (late 16th/early 17th c., likely Italian): steel head totaling 0.576m long including flanges/wings, mounted on ash shaft; main-blade-only portion estimated at 0.42m with the wing-lugs occupying the remaining ~0.16m of head length nearer the socket.

haft_or_grip.material: ash, round section
haft_or_grip.notes: Notably narrower/lighter than dangpa (0.032->but ranseur listed 0.036, still the most thrust-pure of the flanged-partisan family per the audit note) and near the plain war-spear's 0.035m â€” the ranseur is built for clean thrusting, not absorbing heavy blunt impact the way the bear-spear's stouter shaft is.

adornments.label: tassel_or_streamer
adornments.attested: S3 â€” later parade/ceremonial ranseurs and partisans (16th-17th c. guard/court use) are widely depicted with a cloth tassel or streamer at the socket/wing junction in period art and surviving ceremonial examples, but this is a documented feature of the CEREMONIAL/guard-weapon phase of the type's life, not the earlier combative partisan/spetum use; include only as an attested-but-secondary later-period feature, not a combat-functional element.

head_elements.label: central spear point (langet-socketed triangular blade)
head_elements.geom_notes: Long, narrow, diamond or flattened-diamond cross-section triangular thrusting point, langet straps riveted down the socket onto the haft for lateral stiffness against a bind (a documented feature of the family). Tip is acute and needle-like for a percussive thrust; the blade flanks near the socket carry a moderate edge but are not the primary cutting surface -- the point does the work.

head_elements.label: left side prong/wing (forward-curving flange)
head_elements.geom_notes: Slender forged flange curving forward from the socket like a half-bow (Monte's own description), angled toward the tip rather than perpendicular. Edged along its forward-facing convex side (documented reverse/cut use); functions primarily as a bind-catching surface, not a broad chopper -- narrower and thinner-sectioned than the central blade.

head_elements.label: right side prong/wing (forward-curving flange)
head_elements.geom_notes: Mirror of the left wing; the pair sits opposed across the socket, both angled forward and slightly outward. Symmetric mass and geometry with the left flange.

haft_or_grip.material: ash or oak
haft_or_grip.notes: Round-section haft, uniform to slightly tapering toward the socket; no octagonal facets documented for this type (unlike some Chinese/Japanese polearm hafts). Bare wood, no leather wrap documented as standard.

citations: Worcester Art Museum folding spetum (accession not resolved in prior research), 234.5 cm OaL, ~1.5 kg as a ceremonial/collapsible piece -- used only as a length/form anchor, mass adjusted up to a functional war-weight per the cluster note

Pietro Monte (Petrus Montius), Collectanea, Milan 1509, tr. Jeffrey L. Forgeng (Boydell, 2018) -- morphology description: 'a pair of sharp ears, each curving forward like a half-bow', point-primary/wing-cut/wing-parry
Prior cluster research file: clusters/polearm_multi_and_blade_A.json (spetum calibration + audit_note)
provenance.confidence_tier: S2 -- total mass and haft dimensions are typology-typical extrapolation from a documented but atypical (folding/ceremonial) specimen; head morphology (point + two forward-curved wings) is well attested by Monte's period technical description; individual head-element masses are estimated, not directly measured on a war-weight specimen.

head_elements.label: central ox-tongue blade (broad triangular/leaf main blade)
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad, flat, spatulate double-edged blade (ox-tongue/leaf profile), substantially wider at the base than the spetum's narrow point -- Saint Louis Art Museum specimen 32556 gives a 62.9 cm head on a 245.4 cm overall length. Genuinely cuts and thrusts: flat diamond or lenticular cross-section, real sharpened edges running the full blade length, tapering to a functional (not needle-fine) thrusting point.

head_elements.label: left wing-lug (basal flange)
head_elements.geom_notes: Larger, more substantial forged lug than the spetum's wings -- broader flange stock, documented (Manciolino) as an active trap-and-bind catching surface rather than a slashing edge. Set low on the head near the socket, forward-canted.

head_elements.label: right wing-lug (basal flange)
head_elements.geom_notes: Mirror of the left lug; paired basal wings, symmetric.

guards.label: paired wing-lugs (also function as the catching/trap guard)
guards.notes: Mass already counted under head_elements (the wings are simultaneously striking-adjacent morphology and the documented guard/catching structure -- Manciolino states explicitly the wings let the weapon trap where a plain-pointed spear cannot; this guard entry is a structural cross-reference, not additional mass, to avoid double counting.

haft_or_grip.material: ash or oak
haft_or_grip.notes: Round-section haft, slightly thicker than the spetum's to carry the heavier head; langets (side straps) likely riveted from the socket down several centimetres of haft for stiffness under the documented bind-and-retrap cycle, consistent with the family.

citations: Saint Louis Art Museum, accession 32556: overall length 2.454 m, head 62.9 cm, mass 2.8 kg (specimen-measured; used as the primary anchor, mass held slightly below the specimen's 2.8 kg at 2.6 kg per the harmonized calibration)
Antonio Manciolino, Opera Nova, Venice 1531 (Libro 6) -- explicit statement that the partisan/spiedo's wings enable a trap function a plain-pointed spear (quadrello) cannot
Achille Marozzo, Opera Nova, Bologna 1536 -- Punta Portata thrust and parallel cut technique

provenance.confidence_tier: S1 for total mass and overall length (direct specimen measurement); S2 for the head/wing-lug mass split (estimated proportionally from the specimen's total head mass, not independently weighed).

head_elements.label: nagasa (forged sword blade, saki-zori curved single edge)
head_elements.geom_notes: True differentially-forged sword-steel blade (nagasa), typically 40-45 cm on a war-weight naginata, tanged into the nakaye shaft (not socketed). Pronounced saki-zori sweep concentrated toward the tip (the curve is NOT continuous/uniform along the whole blade but sharpest near the point, unlike a katana's more even sori) -- a true draw-cutting geometry with a genuine sharpened edge along the full length and a strong kissaki-equivalent point capable of a targeted thrust (tsuki) to gaps in armor.

haft_or_grip.material: oak (kashi) or similar hardwood
haft_or_grip.notes: Octagonal or round cross-section shaft (nagaye), typically lacquered; tang of the blade is fitted deep into the shaft and pinned (mekugi), unlike the socketed Chinese/European heads in this cluster.

citations: Typological range: nagasa (war blade) ~40-45 cm, nagaye (shaft) ~150-180 cm, total ~2.1 m, war-weight ~1-2 kg -- standard koryu/museum typological convention, not a single named accessioned specimen
Tendo-ryu naginatajutsu (koryu founded 1582) -- 120-kata corpus performed in yoroi, technique pivoting to tsuki/gap-targeted cuts against armor
Toda-ha Buko-ryu naginatajutsu (Ellis Amdur, Koryu.com) -- ishizuki butt-strikes and one-handed shinshikake documented

provenance.confidence_tier: S2 -- typology-typical dimensions and mass are well-established convention across koryu/museum sources, but no single named accessioned specimen with calipers-and-scale measurement was cited in prior research; blade-vs-shaft mass split is estimated.

head_elements.label: leaf-shaped single-edge blade (socketed)
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad single-edged leaf/falchion-like blade, socket-mounted (langet straps riveted down the haft, typical of European polearm sockets -- heavier iron construction than a tanged Japanese nakago). Curvature is slight and concentrated near the tip (mostly-straight spine with a curved cutting tip), deliberately less swept than the naginata's continuous saki-zori. Thicker, less whippy cross-section than the naginata's forged sword blade -- built as an axe-style chopping/cutting head rather than a fine-edged sword blade.

haft_or_grip.material: ash
haft_or_grip.notes: Round-section haft, socket-mounted head with langets. No rear counterweight documented for the European form (unlike the naginata's ishizuki).

citations: Typological range: European single-edge glaive, blade 45-60 cm leaf head, shaft ~120-180 cm, ~2-3.5 kg total -- generic museum-typology convention, no named accessioned specimen cited in prior research

provenance.confidence_tier: S3 -- no period treatise or named accessioned specimen was located in prior research; dimensions and mass rest on generic typological convention and family analogy to the naginata (matched total length and head/grip split, discriminated by mass and curvature only). This is the weakest-confidence weapon in this batch and should be revisited if a genuine glaive-specific specimen or treatise source surfaces.

head_elements.label: main dao blade (deep reclining-moon crescent, single edge)
head_elements.geom_notes: Massive, deeply curved single-edged dao-type cutting blade (Royal Armouries specimen: 253 cm overall, 99 cm blade). Pronounced 'reclining moon' crescent curvature, thick spine tapering to a real but secondary point at the very tip (documented as NOT used as a primary thrusting feature in any period source -- purely a byproduct of the cutting-edge geometry). Cross-section is thick and heavy near the spine (built for sustained chopping impact, unlike a thin fencing blade), edge ground along the full concave face.

head_elements.label: rear spike/hook notch (spine feature at the blade's socket end)
head_elements.geom_notes: A short, stout backward/upward-projecting spike integrated into the blade's spine near the socket, forming a notch between it and the main blade. Documented (S2 typological reasoning) as a hooking/catching feature for controlling an opponent's weapon, shield, or limb -- not a thrusting point, no source documents it as a percussive or piercing striking feature in its own right, purely a catch/hook.

guards.label: socket collar/washer
guards.notes: Small decorative/structural collar or washer at the socket-to-haft junction, sometimes shaped as a stylized beast face on ornate specimens. Not documented as a hand-protecting guard structure in any source -- purely a socket-reinforcement/decorative fitting, distinct in function from a European cross-guard.

haft_or_grip.material: hardwood (often waxwood/gun or similar dense wood), sometimes iron-shod
haft_or_grip.notes: Thick, often octagonal-faceted shaft to resist the torque of the heavy forward-loaded head; grip position sits close to the butt (per the harmonized grip fraction 0.150) to maximize rear-hand leverage against the forward mass.

adornments.label: silk or horsehair tassel at the blade/socket junction
adornments.attested: S2 -- widely attested in period illustrations (e.g. Wujing Zongyao and later Ming/Qing military manual woodcuts) and on surviving Qing-dynasty ceremonial/examination specimens, but not universal on plain military-issue heads; treated as a genuine, attested type-feature for ornamented/ceremonial and higher-status martial specimens rather than every plain field weapon.

citations: Royal Armouries specimen: 253 cm overall length, 99 cm blade (mid-19th-century) -- specimen-measured, primary length/proportion anchor for this cluster

Historical war-weight range ~3-4.5 kg for field/martial specimens, distinct from the legendary/apocryphal 18-48 kg 'Guan Yu weapon' and the graduated 48-83 kg Qing wuju examination-lift specimens (strength-test implements, not combat weapons)
Qi Jiguang, Ji Xiao Xin Shu, 1560/1584 editions -- standardized infantry formation inclusion

The engine's 2.6kg sits inside this replica band, below the single anchor specimen (which the priors judged over-weight for a museum-typical piece). Typology-typical, not a single accessioned specimen.

head_elements.label: dao_blade
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad single-edged curved cleaver blade (sabre-family dao profile), moderate recurve, thin chopping cross-section widening toward a steep clip-point tip; the clip point permits an opportunistic thrust but is not a designed thrusting geometry. Convex edge only, spine thick and unsharpened. No fuller documented.

haft_or_grip.notes: Round-section wooden haft/handle, socketed or tanged into the blade root; no wrap or cross-guard documented for the type. Grip is the rear ~0.33m of the weapon behind the working hand, per the glaive-style rear-grip fraction (not a centre-balanced axe grip).

podao.notes: shaft_grip_estimate_kg here is NOT just the 0.33m rear grip segment -- it is the full-length wood haft/core running the whole 1.82m weapon (32mm diameter, ~1.03kg), since the dao blade is tanged/socketed onto a wood core that continues under the blade's own length, not a bare-metal blade root. Using only the grip-segment length (0.33m -> ~0.19kg) understates the true wood mass and produced a -36.9% residual in an earlier pass; the full-length-core figure brings the budget to -4.6%, within tolerance. Modeling note for Phase B: this cluster's hafted-tip weapons need the shaft/haft part sized to total_length_m, not grip_len_m alone.

fauchard.mass_source: Venetian-style reproduction (By The Sword Inc. commercial repro, ~1.72m haft / 22.5in blade / 2.3kg total, close analog to the engine's 2.2kg) and a distinct fauchard specimen record (22.5in blade, 28.625in blade+socket, 5lb 3.2oz = ~2.36kg). Both cluster around 2.2-2.4kg, supporting the engine value. Typology-typical.

head_elements.label: fauchard_blade
head_elements.geom_notes: Single-edged, pronounced CONCAVE (sickle/scythe-descended) blade -- the edge is on the concave inner curve, unlike a convex sabre-family cutter. Blunt-ish/hook-prone tip (low point concentration), thin cross-section optimized for draw-cuts and hooking pulls rather than thrusting. Long socketed root running most of the shaft's forward length (Metropolitan Museum specimen: rosettes/damascening on ceremonial examples, but plain functional pieces are unadorned).

head_elements.label: back_hook_spike
head_elements.geom_notes: Rear spike/wing/hook attested on the 'composite glaive' fauchard variant (Tzouriadis staff-weapons typology) -- a small forged lug or spike opposite the main edge, used for catching/controlling an opponent's weapon, limb, or a rider's body/harness for a dismounting pull. Not present on the plainest single-edge fauchard forms; attested for the developed composite type only.

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ash-class)
haft_or_grip.notes: Plain round wooden haft, no wrap or cross-guard hardware documented. Long haft (~1.8m functional length behind the blade socket per the Venetian reproduction) gives the weapon its long two-handed reach; grip is not fixed to one spot -- Silver's distance-keeping doctrine implies the hand slides along the haft.

bardiche.mass_source: Cleveland Museum of Art specimen 1916.1563 ('Bardiche (Pole Axe)', cited directly in prior cluster audit) plus Worcester Art Museum specimen 2014.241 (Northern European, possibly Swedish, 1500s-1600s: 138.4cm x 48.3cm, blade 48.3cm long x ~22cm wide, weight ~1.9kg -- a SHORTER-haft variant than the engine's 1.8m total). Royal Armouries object-32545 (late 15th c.) documents a head length of 76.6cm, max width 14.25cm; a related 16th-c. Eastern European crescentic blade measures 74.9cm long. General typology: blade >60cm, total length 180-230cm, weight 1.5-2.5kg, haft ~1.5m. Engine's 2.6kg sits at or slightly above the typical upper band for the longest-bladed examples.

head_elements.label: crescent_blade
head_elements.geom_notes: One extended crescent axe-blade attaching to the haft at TWO points (upper socket near the tip-ward end of the blade run + lower socket/langet near the grip) per the cluster note -- modeled as ONE element spanning the full head_len (1.476m) rather than two separate elements, since it is mechanically a single continuous blade with two haft-attachment points, not two distinct heads. Broad, thin, gently curved (not deeply recurved) axe-bit; edge runs nearly the full length of the element as a sweeping concave-to-straight arc. Essentially no dedicated thrusting point (blunt-ish leading corner), though period sources document opportunistic spear-fashion stabbing with the tip/leading corner through light armor. Cross-section thin and broad (execution-style mass-driven cutting geometry), not a rigid thrusting spike.

guards.label: lower_socket_langet
guards.notes: Strap/langet securing the blade's lower attachment point to the haft near the grip -- structural (anti-shear) hardware, not a hand-protecting guard. Handling research explicitly found no documented hand-protection or bind/catch technique for this feature; it is modeled here purely as the physical strap that realizes the cluster note's 'two-point attachment.'

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ash/oak-class)
haft_or_grip.notes: Unusually SHORT haft relative to blade length (rarely >1.52m of exposed shaft even though total engine length is 1.8m with the blade folded into that span) -- the shortest haft-to-blade ratio of the cluster. Plain round haft, no wrap, no cross-guard. Doubled as a firearm rest/bipod for Streltsy matchlocks (well-documented non-combat handling fact, out of scope for combat primitives but physically real).

bardiche.notes: shaft_grip_estimate_kg uses the full 1.8m wood core at 38mm diameter tapered down from the theoretical max (~1.43kg) to 0.80kg, since a large fraction of the haft's nominal length is displaced/overlapped by the long two-point-mounted blade running alongside it. Residual well within the +/-10% band.

sparr_axe.mass_source: Identified per prior audit as the sparth axe -- the large two-handed Dano-Irish/Hiberno-Norse (gallowglass) broad-axe, Dane-axe-descended (no distinct period 'spar axe' nomenclature found). Wikipedia Dane-axe cross-check: haft 'upwards of 150cm'. Mass estimated from the mid-low war-axe band for this bit size/haft length; no single accessioned specimen weight located in this pass -- treated as typology-typical, weighted toward the low end since the type is documented as a broad THIN bit (execution/cleaving geometry) rather than a thick wedge.

head_elements.label: broad_axe_bit
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad, thin, gently curved-to-triangular crescent bit (less elongated/recurved than the bardiche's blade it evolved into) -- Dane-axe-descended cleaving geometry. No point, no back-spike, no secondary feature; a single-function chopping edge. Thin cross-section for a wide cutting arc, consistent with felling/cleaving blows against men, horses, and mail described in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and Bayeux Tapestry record of its ancestor.

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ash-class)
haft_or_grip.notes: Plain round haft, no rondel, no langets, no cross-guard -- handling research confirms bare-haft construction throughout the type's documented and reconstructed record. Shortest weapon of the cluster (1.5m total).

citations: Wikipedia, 'Dane axe' (sparth haft length 'upwards of 150cm')
Anglo-Saxon Chronicle + Bayeux Tapestry (documented ancestor use) [S1]
Thompson, Grosse, Pfrenger, Gustitis -- Scorners of Death: Fighting Skills of the Medieval Gaelic Warrior (Fallen Rook Publishing, 2018) [S2]
designs/scene/combat_engine_v1/weapons.py sparr_axe record (mass=2.3, head_len=4.1, grip_len=0.9, haft_d=0.038)
provenance.confidence_tier: S3

sparr_axe.notes: shaft_grip_estimate_kg uses the full 1.5m wood core at 38mm diameter (theoretical max ~1.19kg, trimmed slightly to 1.10kg for taper toward the grip end). Residual within the +/-10% band.

voulge.mass_source: Modern functional replicas and typology sources converge on shaft length 1.5-2m (military voulge ~180cm), blade 50-80cm, matching the engine's 2.0m total. Weight sources diverge more: some accounts give ~5lb (~2.3kg) for a complete piece, while an isolated head-only reconstruction weighs 0.74kg for a 460mm blade -- the engine's 2.2kg total (head+haft) is consistent with the ~5lb whole-weapon figure, not the head-only figure. No handling/treatise research was available for this weapon in the prior pass (primary_sources empty) -- physical facts here rest on typology/replica sources only, not technique-attested specimens.

head_elements.label: cleaver_blade
head_elements.geom_notes: Broad, roughly straight-backed cleaving edge (Wikipedia/typology: 'broad blade meant for hacking', distinguishing it from the glaive's narrower cutting blade) mounted by binding the lower two-thirds of the blade to the side of the pole (not a true socket the way a spearhead is socketed) -- a documented attachment-method distinct from the fauchard/bardiche's socket-based mounting. Slight curvature toward the tip. Point present but blunt/utilitarian relative to a spear.

head_elements.label: thrusting_heel_spike
head_elements.geom_notes: Reinforced thrusting point/heel at the forward-most tip, distinct from the cleaving edge proper -- the calibration's basis for the weapon's cut_thrust (rather than pure straight_cut) classification: a broad cleaver PLUS a reinforced point, not a single-mode edge. Roughly in-line with the haft axis for thrust delivery (orient_deg near 0, vs the blade's swing-presented ~30deg).

head_elements.label: rear_fluke
head_elements.geom_notes: Small rearward spur/fluke opposite the main edge, documented in the calibration audit note as a minor concentrated feature (captured in the deterministic-physics layer as strike_concentration=0.10). Not large enough to reclassify the head away from cut_thrust; functions as an incidental hook/catch point rather than a designed hammer or beak.

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ash-class)
haft_or_grip.notes: Round wooden haft/pole with the blade's lower two-thirds bound alongside it (strap/socket-hybrid mounting per typology sources), rather than a pure end-socket. No cross-guard or wrap documented. No handling research available to confirm choke-up/grip-shift technique -- grip dynamics here are inferred from geometry alone (flagged lower-confidence).

citations: Wikipedia, 'Voulge' (shaft 1.5-2m; military voulge 180cm; blade 50-80cm; binding attachment of lower two-thirds of blade to shaft, distinguishing it from the glaive)
Malevus, 'Voulge: This Weapon Created the First Regular French Army'
Medieval Depot / Knights Edge, 14th Century Voulge Polearm functional replicas

NOTE: no dedicated period fencing treatise or technique-attested specimen located for the voulge in either the prior research pass or this one -- physical facts rest on general typology/replica sources only, the weakest-attested weapon in this batch

guisarme.grip_split_note: Engine calibration: head_len 5.7u + grip_len 1.3u (UNIT_M=0.30) = 2.10m total; grip fraction 0.186 (~spear's 0.18). Working-hand grip point taken at x_m=0, located 0.39m from the butt end.

head_elements.label: hooked cutting blade
head_elements.geom_notes: Single-edged, forward-curving hook blade riveted/socketed to the haft, shallow concave curvature on the inner (hook) edge, convex cutting edge on the outer face; point at the extreme tip is drawn out for a weak secondary thrust. Cross-section is a flattened wedge, thinner and lighter than a full spearhead â€” the museum-replica consensus this batch's calibration cites gives a curved head roughly 30 cm long. Edge present along the full outer curve; inner hook face is typically unsharpened or only lightly honed (its job is catching, not cutting).

head_elements.label: reverse thrusting spike
head_elements.geom_notes: Short straight spike mounted opposite the hook, in-line with the haft axis, for the weapon's secondary thrust action; described in sources as an 'afterthought' relative to the main hook blade â€” slender, square or diamond cross-section, sharply pointed.

haft_or_grip.material: ash/oak
haft_or_grip.notes: Long, uniform-taper round haft (spear-typical), no octagonal facets documented; full length grippable/gatherable (grip_choke_max=1.0 per engine calibration) since the hook sits only at the very tip.

ji.grip_split_note: Engine calibration: head_len 6.5u + grip_len 1.5u (UNIT_M=0.30) = 2.40m total; grip fraction 0.188. Working-hand grip point at x_m=0, located 0.45m from the butt end. Sources describe a 'Combat Ji' shaft near 2.3m and 'Battalion Ji' longer for formation use; 2.40m sits at the upper end of the combat-length band.

head_elements.label: straight spearhead (thrusting point)
head_elements.geom_notes: Single-piece forge-welded (Han-era T-shaped ji) leaf/willow-leaf-profile spearhead in-line with the haft axis, socketed onto the shaft; comparable LK Chen swallow-tail spearhead components run ~150g at ~23.6cm length for a spear-only head, consistent with this element's estimate. Sharp thrusting point, diamond or lenticular cross-section, edges present but the point is the primary engaged feature.

head_elements.label: perpendicular crescent (yueyadao) blade
head_elements.geom_notes: Crescent/dagger-axe-style blade mounted perpendicular (or slightly angled) below the spearhead, riveted to a single forged piece in the Han T-shaped ji. Outer curve is a cutting edge (chop/slash action); inner curve is the hooking surface used to unhorse cavalry, catch shields, or disarm â€” described across S1 sources as a coequal offensive structure, not a guard. Curved profile, moderate curvature, edge along the full outer arc.

guards.label: crescent horns (incidental hand-catching)
guards.geom_notes: Not a separate mass element â€” the crescent blade itself (listed under head_elements) doubles as the weapon's only guard-like structure, providing incidental hand protection during binds per S2 synthesis sources. No dedicated hilt-level guard hardware exists on this weapon; recorded here for completeness of the catching-structure inventory, mass already counted under the crescent head element (not double-counted in the mass budget).

haft_or_grip.material: ash/oak (Han-era shafts also attested in bamboo-laminate/hardwood composite)
haft_or_grip.notes: Long uniform round haft; full length grippable/gatherable (grip_choke_max=1.0) â€” the crescent sits at the tip, outside the gather zone.

bec_de_corbin.grip_split_note: Engine calibration: head_len 3.0u + grip_len 3.0u (UNIT_M=0.30), 50/50 centre-grip split = 1.80m total, matching the direct poleaxe analog. Working-hand grip point (nominal, mid-haft per half-shafting doctrine) at x_m=0, located 0.90m from the butt end. Met specimen (see citations) runs longer overall (202.9cm) at a heavier total mass (2.6-3.4kg by source) â€” the engine's 1.80m/2.4kg anchor sits toward the lighter/shorter end of the attested specimen range; treated as within typology variance, not overridden.

head_elements.label: hammer face
head_elements.geom_notes: Flat or lightly-pyramided square/rectangular striking face mounted perpendicular to the haft, opposite the beak, for blunt concussive strikes to the helm. Documented (Fiore, Le Jeu de la Hache, Talhoffer) as the secondary striking mode relative to the beak on this particular typological variant ('used primarily with the beak instead of the hammer' per this cluster's own audit note). Squat, blocky cross-section; no edge.

head_elements.label: curved beak (bec de corbin)
head_elements.geom_notes: Sharply down-curving, tapering beak/pick opposite the hammer face â€” the weapon's namesake and primary documented striking element on this variant. Function is hooking/tearing/puncturing mail and gaps rather than a clean edge-cut (no cutting edge documented); thick at the root, drawn to a point at the tip, pronounced downward curvature ('crow's beak' profile).

head_elements.label: top spike
head_elements.geom_notes: Straight spike projecting from the crown of the head, in-line with the haft axis, for controlled thrusts into visor slits, armpits, and joints at half-shaft range. Slender, square or diamond cross-section, sharp point; documented as the weapon's dedicated 'point' function (distinct from the beak's hook/tear role).

guards.label: rondel disc
guards.geom_notes: Small circular steel disc fitted just below the head, documented on period poleaxes of this class to protect the leading hand from a sliding strike down the haft. Passive/structural, not an actively-worked parrying surface.

guards.label: langets
guards.geom_notes: Two or four narrow steel straps running down the haft from the head socket, riveted flush to the wood. Documented function is reinforcing the haft against being cut through in a bind, not weapon-catching.

haft_or_grip.material: ash/oak
haft_or_grip.notes: Round-to-octagonal haft (period poleaxe hafts are frequently faceted for grip); full length grippable (grip_choke_max=1.0), consistent with documented half-shafting (grip sliding to mid-haft for close-range bind work).

head_elements.label: hammer face (4-tine fluke, striking side)
head_elements.geom_notes: Flat or lightly-domed square striking face opposite the fluke, for blunt concussive blows. Met specimen head measures 18 7/8 in / 48 cm long by 8 in / 20.4 cm wide overall (hammer+fluke+spike combined) â€” this element is the compact striking-face sub-portion of that head. No edge; blocky cross-section.

head_elements.label: 3-4 tine fluke (rear beak)
head_elements.geom_notes: Distinctive multi-pronged (typically 3-4 tine) fluke opposite the hammer face â€” the Lucerne hammer's namesake feature, distinguishing it from the bec de corbin's single curved beak. Documented (Wikipedia/HandWiki/Met) as a broader, less concentrated striking/hooking structure than a single beak: the tines spread the strike over a wider area (this cluster's own audit note: strike_concentration 0.62, below bec_de_corbin's 0.80). Each tine tapers to a blunt-to-lightly-pointed tip; no continuous cutting edge.

head_elements.label: top spike
head_elements.geom_notes: Straight spike projecting from the crown, in-line with the haft axis; documented across Le Jeu de la Hache and Fiore's azza plays as the weapon's primary thrust-delivery element (estocq favored over swinging tour de bras strikes) â€” thrust-primary per handling research, though the engine represents this via elevated point_concentration within a 'blunt' composite head rather than a head-token change (per the harmonization note preserving the poleaxe-sibling model). katana.grip_head_split_note: Engine calibration (harmonized): head_len 2.333 units x UNIT_M 0.30 = 0.6999 m blade-side extent from x=0 (working hand); grip_len 0.9 units = 0.2700 m tsuka behind x=0. Matches specimen nagasa-to-tsuka ratio (~70cm:27cm, tsuka ~1/3 nagasa).

head_elements.label: blade (nagasa)
head_elements.geom_notes: Single-edge curved blade (shinogi-zukuri cross-section: ridgeline dividing a convex edge-side bevel from a flatter back), differentially hardened (hamon visible at edge). Tapered point (kissaki) of moderate length (chu-kissaki typical), acute enough for a genuine thrust but not needle-like. Sori (curvature) concentrated toward the tsuba-to-monouchi region for a katana mounted edge-up (uchigatana style), averaging ~1.5cm deflection over the ~70cm chord (~2%), shallower than a cavalry sabre. Centroid placed at 42% of blade length from the guard (x=0.294m of a 0.70m blade) reflecting mass concentrated in the wider, thicker proximal 2/3 (moto-haba/moto-kasane), tapering to a thin, light distal section (saki-haba/saki-kasane).

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ho wood/magnolia) core, wrapped in same-skin (rayskin/samegawa) and silk or cotton ito cord in a criss-cross wrap
haft_or_grip.notes: Slight forward-to-rear taper; core is a two-piece wood shell around the tang (nakago), pinned with one or two bamboo mekugi pegs; ito wrap adds surface grip texture, not a structural diameter change.

tachi.grip_head_split_note: Engine calibration (harmonized): head_len 2.6 units x 0.30 = 0.7800 m blade extent; grip_len 0.933 units = 0.2799 m tsuka. Matches specimen tachi nagasa range 70-80cm (0.78m taken as mid-representative) with proportionally slightly longer tsuka than katana for two-hand use afoot.

head_elements.label: blade (nagasa)
head_elements.geom_notes: Single-edge curved blade, shinogi-zukuri cross-section, differentially hardened. Deeper sori than a katana -- curvature concentrated nearer the tang/koshi-zori style (curve set low, closer to the guard), consistent with an edge-down horseback mount favoring a sweeping draw-cut. Attested sori on tachi specimens runs roughly 3cm over a 78cm chord (~4%), roughly double the katana's. Point is typically narrower/more tapered (smaller kissaki, greater taper from moto-haba to saki-haba) than a katana per tachi-vs-katana typology descriptions (built more for cutting through/past armor lamellae than for a broad cutting edge). Centroid placed at 42% of blade length from guard.

guards.label: tsuba
guards.material: steel/iron (earlier tachi fittings often plainer/simpler than later elaborate koshirae; high-status ceremonial tachi carry gilt-bronze kazari-tachi mounts, excluded here as non-combat)

haft_or_grip.material: wood (ho wood) core, samegawa (rayskin) wrap, ito cord binding
haft_or_grip.notes: Tachi koshirae carries two hangers (ashi) on the scabbard for edge-down horizontal suspension -- a scabbard-mount detail, not part of the tsuka itself. Grip proportionally similar taper to katana, slightly longer to suit two-handed use when fought on foot.

head_elements.label: blade (flame-ground section, forte-to-mid)
head_elements.geom_notes: The wavy/flame-ground section: alternating concave-convex undulations ground into both edges over roughly the proximal two-thirds of the blade (forte through mid-blade), leaving the distal third a plain straight edge (see 'blade tip' element below); flat hexagonal or lenticular cross-section typical of the Zweihander/spadone family; the wave is a lateral edge-grinding pattern, not a change to the blade's overall lateral curvature (which remains straight/zero, matching the greatsword baseline) â€” it does not alter cut or thrust geometry per the documented technical corpus, which treats the flamberge identically to a straight-bladed Zweihander in use.

head_elements.label: blade tip (plain distal section + Parierhaken zone)
head_elements.geom_notes: Distal third of the blade, ground plain/straight (undulation stops before the tip on documented specimens), tapering to an acute point; on many surviving Zweihander/flamberge specimens a pair of Parierhaken (hooked side-lugs) is mounted at the base of this section, just above the ricasso, projecting laterally to catch an opponent's polearm shaft in a bind.

head_elements.label: ricasso (blunted forward gripping section)
head_elements.geom_notes: Deliberately dulled/unsharpened blade section immediately forward of the main guard, meant to be gripped bare- or gauntlet-handed for half-swording (Halbschwert) at close range; rectangular/flattened cross-section, not part of the cutting edge.

Cross-cluster comparability: these five plus the canonical 'spear' form the full two-handed thrust-spear family -- all share grip fraction ~0.12-0.19 and shaft-mass-by-cylinder-volume methodology; mass scale runs 1.23kg (yari, lightest/dueling) to 2.35kg (bear_spear, stoutest/impact-rated), which is coherent with their documented use-cases (duelling vs. charge-reception). || ranseur (polearm_spears_A): adornment 'tassel_or_streamer' is S3-attested (period art/ceremonial specimens) but explicitly non-combat-functional per its own source cluster -- RETAINED (attested, not speculative) but flagged as decorative/ceremonial-phase only, not a combat physical fact. || polearm_multi_and_blade_A (spetum, partisan, naginata, glaive, guandao): schema already close to canonical (pommel/butt pre-split). Re-derived shaft_or_grip_est_kg as sum_kg minus head/guard/pommel/butt sums for internal consistency (source gave shaft mass only as free text inside 'components'). guandao carries an attested tassel adornment (S2, period illustration + Qing ceremonial specimens) -- RETAINED per instruction 6 since it is attested, though flagged non-universal on plain field pieces. || polearm_choppers_A (podao, fauchard, bardiche, sparr_axe, voulge): schema already close to canonical. Cross-cluster note: bardiche (1.75kg bit) and sparr_axe (1.15kg bit) are both Dane-axe-descended broad choppers of comparable haft length (1.8m/1.5m) -- bardiche's bit is heavier consistent with its longer/wider crescent blade (S1-specimen-grounded); sparr_axe's lighter bit and shorter haft are internally coherent for a one-and-a-half-hand gallowglass axe (S3, no specimen mass located). No outliers requiring correction. || polearm_hooks_hammers_A (guisarme, ji, bec_de_corbin, lucerne_hammer, goedendag): bec_de_corbin and lucerne_hammer are directly comparable poleaxe-family siblings (same 1.80m/50-50 centre-grip split, same haft diameter 0.040m) -- good cross-cluster comparability with the canonical roster's 'poleaxe' entry (2.5kg, 1.8m implied). ji's crescent blade doubles as head_element AND guard with shared (non-duplicated) mass -- consistent pattern with the canonical roster's poleaxe langets/disc guard treatment. || sword_japanese_chinese_long_A (katana, tachi, odachi, tsurugi, changdao): schema close to canonical, field renamed 'kashira'->pommel (same slot). Good cross-cluster comparability: katana/tachi/odachi share identical construction family (shinogi-zukuri, tsuba+kashira), scaling coherently 1.1kg->1.2kg->2.2kg with blade length 0.70->0.78->1.20m. tsurugi (S1 whole-sword anchor, 0.65kg) and changdao (1.6kg, S1 treatise-specified length) are outliers by design (different eras/families), not flagged. || sword_curved_world_sabres_A (nandao, jian, scimitar, pulwar, shamshir): good cross-cluster comparability with existing_roster's canonical 'sabre' (0.9kg, 0.78m blade, curvature 0.42) -- scimitar (1.0kg/0.855m, curvature ~implied higher via yalman) and shamshir (0.85kg/0.81m, roster-max curvature 0.70) form a coherent curved-cutter ladder alongside it. jian carries an attested pommel tassel (S2, civilian/taijijian tradition) -- RETAINED per instruction 6, flagged non-combat/ceremonial (matches ranseur/guandao's tassel pattern from the polearm clusters). || sword_straight_thrust_and_broad_A (szabla, cinquedea, flamberge, estoc, estoc_halfsword, falchion): schema already close to canonical with components given as a nested dict. cinquedea is correctly base-heavy (centroid at 30% of head length, x_m=0.135 of 0.45m head) reflecting its documented wide-base/rapid-taper geometry -- cross-checked against dagger cluster's rondel/misericorde (which instead centre their mass at 50% of head length) and found to be a deliberate, well-justified deviation, not an inconsistency. || dagger_and_hook_A (rondel, main_gauche, stiletto, misericorde, hook_sword): normalized pommel_or_butt -> pommel{}/butt{} split matching canonical schema. Good cross-cluster comparability: rondel/misericorde/stiletto form a coherent point-pure dagger ladder (0.22-0.35kg) sharing the disc-guard-plus-pommel-disc construction pattern; main_gauche's guard mass (0.18kg, ~33% of total weapon mass) is a deliberate, well-documented outlier (Fabris/Capoferro/Giganti-attested parrying function) not a defect. hook_sword is the weakest-grounded weapon across the ENTIRE roster (S3, no period treatise, scaled from a single antique dealer specimen) -- flagged for follow-up. || CROSS-CLUSTER sabre family comparability (sabre, scimitar, pulwar, shamshir, szabla, nandao): masses range 0.85-1.3kg, blade lengths 0.78-0.87m, all single-curved-blade + guard + grip + pommel four-part decomposition. Coherent as a family; nandao is the heaviest (1.3kg) reflecting its thicker niuweidao-lineage chopper cross-section (deliberate, sourced) -- no outliers requiring correction. || CROSS-CLUSTER hammer/pick composite comparability (mace, poleaxe, bec_de_corbin, lucerne_hammer): poleaxe/bec_de_corbin/lucerne_hammer share the same three-element compound-head pattern (blunt face + hook/beak/fluke + top spike) and near-identical haft geometry (1.8m, 50/50 centre-grip split, ~0.038-0.040m diameter) -- excellent comparability, consistent with all three being poleaxe-family weapons. mace is a simpler single-flanged-block head at a shorter overall length (implied ~0.74m from head_len/grip_len ratios), correctly NOT sharing the three-element pattern since it lacks a beak/spike. No outliers.
